title: Qatar LNG Plant Drone Attack Triggers Global Energy Shock
content:
The global economy is facing renewed pressure as the risks of higher inflation and higher unemployment both increase. The combination of higher oil prices and a weak job market hammered stocks and bonds, lifting market volatility to its highest levels in nearly a year. The S&P 500 index was down 2% for the week, near its lowest levels since mid-December. The Nasdaq Composite gave back 1.2%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average slumped 3%.

Despite these warning signs, the U.S. administration remains optimistic, projecting full-year economic growth around 4%. However, the current consensus on Wall Street is for a figure closer to 2.2%. Both forecasts could prove overly optimistic in a stagnant labor market combined with a prolonged energy price shock. The market for liquefied natural gas (LNG), which serves as the backbone of Europe’s and Asia’s electricity and heating supplies, is particularly vulnerable. LNG depends heavily on Middle Eastern suppliers who compress, freeze, and ship the gas globally, but critical supply chains are currently paralyzed.

Qatar, which accounts for about 20% of the global LNG supply, recently shut down its main plant following a drone attack from Iran. Even if operations resume, transporting energy products out of the Persian Gulf is nearly impossible because the Strait of Hormuz is effectively blocked. Consequently, European LNG prices surged by 67% in a single week, marking the sharpest increase since 2022. This disruption has been a temporary boon to U.S. exporters of LNG.

The conflict could also fundamentally alter the long-term decisions that LNG buyers make. Qatar typically sells its LNG through long-term contracts with end buyers, but its reliance on the vulnerable Strait of Hormuz means that buyers may think twice before signing new contracts. For energy companies building new power plants, securing a stable and reliable supply chain has suddenly become their most pressing concern.
